narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and an LSAT score of 33, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in others that the admissions model couldn't overlook. The model predicts a 61.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record compared to thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average—this signals to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 actually worked in your favor, as it's stronger than the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your LSAT score of 33, however, ranked third in influence and nudged things toward failure, since it's below the dataset average of 36.70 for students who typically succeed. Other details like your upper-middle family income and full-time student status played smaller roles and aligned with the norm, where over 92% are full-time like you. In essence, while your solid undergrad grades show real promise, the model weighed your weaker later high school decile and LSAT most heavily against you, seeing them as stronger predictors of bar risk than your strengths. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile is close on GPA but lags on those key metrics that best forecast first-time bar passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's the model's data-driven call based on patterns from past applicants. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through additional coursework if possible, and applying to schools with more holistic reviews that might value your full-time commitment and background. You could also seek feedback from admissions counselors or prep programs tailored to bar success stories like yours. With targeted improvements, a stronger application is within reach next cycle.
